topic: A person's invention is already patented by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the embossed seal on the official document with a trembling finger. The ink seemed to swim beneath his gaze, the lines of the patent claim mocking his sleepless nights. He stared at the bold lettering—*Utility Patent No. 88392*. His prototype, the 'Chrono-Lattice,' which promised to revolutionize temporal data storage, was undeniably his. Yet, the paperwork confirmed another name, another inventor, holding the keys to his creation. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, pulling at his diaphragm. He pushed the papers away, the sound sharp in the quiet workshop. He needed air. He walked to the window, the city lights blurring through the condensation on the glass, feeling a sudden, profound emptiness settle over him.

[story 2]

"It’s impossible," Lena muttered, running a hand repeatedly through her already disheveled hair. She leaned over the schematics spread across the workbench, her breath catching in shallow gasps. The counter-signature, the date stamp—everything pointed to Marcus Thorne. Her heart hammered against her ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. She picked up the physical copy of the filing, the paper feeling flimsy and utterly inadequate against the weight of the decision. A flush crept up her neck, staining her cheeks. She squeezed her eyes shut, trying to force a clear thought, but all she could perceive was the suffocating realization that the breakthrough she’d labored over was already claimed.

[story 3]

The silence in the lab was deafening. Arthur paced the length of the room three times, his boots scuffing the concrete floor with rhythmic, agitated taps. He stopped abruptly by the display case holding the Chrono-Lattice prototype. He didn't touch it; instead, he circled it slowly, his jaw tight, his knuckles white where he gripped his own forearms. Every glance at the patent documentation sent a jolt of nausea through him. He reread the section detailing the prior art, his eyes scanning the fine print until they blurred. A deep, shaky exhale escaped him, a sound laced with utter defeat. He sank onto a stool, staring blankly at the wall, unable to summon the energy to move.
